metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — signed 'Your loving son, Bob'; return address on envelope reads 'Cpl. Boffangen [Hanifen?] 17060[0], Sqd. 1 - BPS-279, Chanute Fld. Ill.'
metadata.to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 904 Des Way N. [?], Des Moines, Iowa — likely addressed to James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad)
metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 904 Des [Moines] Way N., Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.envelope_notes: Marked 'Free' in upper right corner (military free mail). Return address: Cpl. B[ob] [H]anifen, 17060[0?], Sqd. 1 - BPS-279, Chanute Fld. Ill. Some ink scrawls/marks on front of envelope. The surname on the return address is partially obscured but consistent with Hanifen.
topics: new shoes helped on the trip
no trouble from camp about absence
plans to come home again this week
metadata.mood_tone: Light, casual, and upbeat; Bob is relieved that he got back on time and avoided any repercussions from camp, and looks forward to coming home again soon.
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is somewhat hurried and cursive; a few words required interpretation. Return address on envelope partially unclear. 'Sqd.' and unit number on envelope are best guesses. '904 Des Way N.' in recipient address is uncertain — could be a street address abbreviation.
metadata.historical_context: Written September 1, 1945, just days after V-J Day (August 15, 1945) and the formal end of WWII (September 2, 1945). Bob is stationed at Chanute Field, Illinois, a U.S. Army Air Forces training base. The 'Free' mail designation indicates active military service. The relaxed tone suggests the war's end and anticipation of discharge/return home.

pages.transcription:
[Return address, upper left]
Cpl. B[ob] [H]anifen, 170600
Sqd. 1 - BPS-279
Chanute Fld. Ill.

[Postmark, center]
RANTOUL, ILL.
SEP 11
3:30 PM
1945

[Upper right]
Free

[Recipient address, center]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Des [Moines?] Way N.
Des Moines, Iowa

Sept. 1, 45

Dear Dad
  We had a swell time and got back on time. Never had a bit of trouble, those new shoes really helped a lot. We got off okay at Camp no body said a word to us yet, so it looks like we came out ahead I hope. Not a lot to write about around here same old place. Not doing a lot in the shop. I will get Edith a present today hope she will like it. I guess

I will be home again this week as long as everything goes right. to expect me anytime
Well Dad goodbye for now.

Your loving son
  Bob

full_transcription:
Sept. 1, 45

Dear Dad
  We had a swell time and got back on time. Never had a bit of trouble, those new shoes really helped a lot. We got off okay at Camp no body said a word to us yet, so it looks like we came out ahead I hope. Not a lot to write about around here same old place. Not doing a lot in the shop. I will get Edith a present today hope she will like it. I guess I will be home again this week as long as everything goes right. to expect me anytime
Well Dad goodbye for now.

Your loving son
  Bob
summary: Written September 1, 1945, from Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob writes a brief note to his father (Dad) reporting that he had a great visit home, got back to camp on time, and that new shoes made the trip easier. He notes no one at camp said anything about their absence, so he hopes they came out ahead. He plans to get Edith a present and expects to come home again later that week. The letter is short and upbeat, reflecting the relaxed atmosphere near the end of WWII.
